ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'chicken tikka' class (FOODON_03302051); Murgh Chandi Tikka is a silver/white marinade variant not separately coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Murgh Chandi Tikka or chicken tikka as a prepared dish. A00LC (Chicken meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Murgh Chandi Tikka or a sufficiently close prepared chicken tikka concept.

main_ingredients: silver leaf (chandi vark) or white marinade
allergens: gluten (possible if flour used in marinade)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Murgh Chandi Tikka is approximately 200 g, comprising 4 to 5 boneless chicken cubes marinated in a white cream-yoghurt spice blend and cooked in a tandoor or charcoal oven.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken tikka (tandoori-style, boneless, cooked) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central entry for roasted chicken breast with marinade
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition); USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Murgh Chandi Tikka is a white-marinade variant of chicken tikka. ICMR-NIN values for tandoori chicken / chicken tikka (boneless, cooked) were used as the primary basis. The white marinade (cream, yoghurt, white pepper, cardamom) slightly elevates fat and energy compared to a plain tandoori chicken; values adjusted upward by approximately 10–15% for fat and energy to reflect the cream-enriched marinade. USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 171477, Chicken breast, boneless, cooked, roasted) used as a cross-check for protein and micronutrients.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of protein-dominant grilled/tandoori chicken dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for cooked chicken and high-protein low-carbohydrate meat dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Murgh Chandi Tikka. The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI values of approximately 0–35 for plain cooked chicken and high-protein, low-carbohydrate meat dishes. A GI of 30 is assigned as a conservative analogous estimate reflecting the very low carbohydrate content (2.8 g per 100 g) and the protein-dominant macronutrient profile. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (30 × 5.6 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.7, confirming negligible glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~840 mg per 200 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A 200 g serving of Murgh Chandi Tikka contributes approximately 840 mg sodium, representing about 36% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and avoidance of additional high-sodium condiments such as chutneys or pickles is advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index (~30)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Murgh Chandi Tikka has a very low carbohydrate content of approximately 2.8 g per 100 g and an estimated glycemic index of 30, resulting in a glycemic load of only 1.7 per serving, making it a suitable high-protein option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as naan or rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~3.2 g per 100 g) and sodium (~420 mg per 100 g) from cream-enriched marinade
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The cream and yoghurt marinade in Murgh Chandi Tikka contributes approximately 3.2 g saturated fat per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 150 g or fewer and pair with non-starchy vegetables to keep saturated fat intake within the recommended 20 g daily ceiling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Murgh Chandi Tikka is a refined North Indian and Mughlai grilled chicken dish in which boneless chicken cubes (typically breast or thigh) are marinated in a delicate white or silver marinade composed of thick yoghurt, fresh cream, ginger-garlic paste, white pepper, cardamom, mace, and lemon juice, then cooked on skewers in a tandoor (clay oven) or over charcoal. The name 'Chandi' (Hindi: चाँदी) means silver, referring either to the pale ivory colour of the marinade or, in its most elaborate restaurant presentation, to the application of edible silver leaf (vark) over the finished tikka. The dish is characterised by a tender, juicy interior, a lightly charred exterior, and a mild, aromatic flavour profile that is subtler and creamier than the more intensely spiced red tandoori chicken tikka. Murgh Chandi Tikka is a popular starter or appetiser in Indian fine-dining restaurants and is served with mint chutney, sliced onion rings, and lemon wedges. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish providing approximately 22.5 g protein and only 2.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g, with a moderate fat content of 9.5 g per 100 g largely attributable to the cream and yoghurt marinade. Its estimated glycemic index of approximately 30 and glycemic load of under 2 per serving make it highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g warrants attention for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. In Singapore, this dish is served at Indian restaurants and is available on food delivery platforms including field survey.

ingredient_synonyms.tandoor: clay oven used in South Asian cooking
ingredient_synonyms.vark: edible silver leaf used as garnish

confidence_reason: Murgh Chandi Tikka is a named restaurant variant of chicken tikka not individually listed in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN tandoori chicken / chicken tikka data with an upward adjustment for the cream-enriched white marinade. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked chicken. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (tandoori chicken / chicken tikka, boneless, cooked) with approximately 10–15% upward adjustment to fat and energy to reflect the cream-enriched white marinade characteristic of Chandi Tikka. USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 171477) used as a cross-check for protein and micronutrients. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central roasted chicken breast data and ICMR-NIN composite values; these are reasonable estimates but not directly measured for this specific dish. 4. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Murgh Chandi Tikka was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. The dish is served at Indian House / Dumpukht Grills & Curry, Boat Quay, Singapore, as sourced from field survey data. 6. Edible silver leaf (vark) is sometimes applied as garnish; this does not materially affect macronutrient values. 7. Halal status is assumed based on the restaurant context (Indian Muslim-style restaurant); kosher status is not applicable.
